en: Yellow Zhi (yellow reishi fungus). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for the five pathogens of the heart and abdomen; boosts spleen qi, calms the spirit, and brings loyalty, trustworthiness, and harmony. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life like an immortal. Also called Golden Zhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

White Zhi. Taste pungent, nature neutral. Indicated for cough with rising qi; boosts lung qi, opens and clears the mouth and nose, strengthens the will and courage, and calms the corporeal soul (po). Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life like an immortal. Also called Jade Zhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Black Zhi. Taste salty, nature neutral. Indicated for dribbling urinary blockage; disinhibits the water passages, boosts kidney qi, opens the nine orifices, and sharpens hearing and perception. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life like an immortal. Also called Mysterious Zhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Purple Zhi. Taste sweet, nature warm. Indicated for deafness; benefits the joints, preserves the spirit, boosts essence-qi, strengthens the sinews and bones, and improves complexion. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life like an immortal. Also called Wood Zhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Tianmendong (Cochinchinese asparagus root). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for sudden wind-damp hemilateral bi-syndrome (numbness and paralysis); strengthens the bones and marrow, kills the three worms, and expels hidden corpse-influx. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, boosts qi, and prolongs life. Also called Dianle. Grows in mountain valleys.

Zhu (Atractylodes). Taste bitter, nature warm. Indicated for damp bi-syndrome, necrotic flesh, tetany, and jaundice; stops sweating, clears heat, aids digestion, and can be prepared as a decoction or pill. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prolongs life, and dispels hunger. Also called Mountain Thistle. Grows in the mountain valleys of Mount Zheng.

Weiwei (Fragrant Solomon's Seal, Polygonatum odoratum). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for wind-stroke and sudden heat causing immobility, torn sinews and knotted flesh, and various deficiencies. Taken long-term, it removes dark blemishes on the face, gives the complexion a healthy sheen, and lightens the body while preventing aging. Grows in river valleys.

Dried Rehmannia Root. Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for fractures, sprains, severed sinews, and internal injury; expels blood bi-syndrome, fills the bone marrow, and builds flesh. Made into a decoction, it removes cold-heat accumulations and bi-syndrome. The fresh root is even better. Also called Marrow of the Earth. Grows in river marshes.

Changpu (Acorus, Sweet Flag). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome and cough with rising qi; opens the orifices of the heart, supplements the five viscera, opens the nine orifices, sharpens hearing and vision, and clears the voice. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, improves memory, dispels confusion, and prolongs life. Also called Changyang. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Yuanzhi (Polygala Root). Taste bitter, nature warm. Indicated for cough with rising qi and internal injury; supplements deficiency, expels pathogenic qi, disinhibits the nine orifices, sharpens intelligence, improves hearing and vision, aids memory, and strengthens the mind and doubles one's strength. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prevents aging. Its leaves are called Xiaocao (Small Herb). Also called Yaorao or Xicao. Grows in river valleys.

Zexie (Alisma). Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome and difficult lactation; disperses water retention, nourishes the five viscera, boosts qi and strength, and promotes healthy weight. Taken long-term, it sharpens hearing and vision, dispels hunger, prolongs life, lightens the body, gives the face a healthy glow, and, it is said, enables one to walk on water. Also called Shuixie, Mangyu, or Huxie. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Shuyu (Chinese Yam, Dioscorea). Taste sweet, slightly warm. Indicated for internal injury; supplements deficiency and emaciation, removes cold-heat pathogenic qi, supplements the center and boosts qi and strength, and builds flesh. Taken long-term, it sharpens hearing and vision, lightens the body, dispels hunger, and prolongs life. Also called Mountain Yam. Grows in mountain valleys.

Juhua (Chrysanthemum Flower). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for wind affecting the head, dizziness with swelling and pain, eyes that feel as if bulging out, tearing, necrotic and wasted skin, and aversion to wind-damp bi-syndrome. Taken long-term, it benefits the blood and qi, lightens the body, and resists aging while prolonging life. Also called Jiehua. Grows in river marshes.

Gancao (Licorice Root). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for cold-heat pathogenic qi in the five viscera and six bowels; strengthens the sinews and bones, builds flesh, doubles one's strength, treats wounds from metal blades and swelling of the legs, and resolves toxicity. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prolongs life. Grows in river valleys.

Renshen (Ginseng). Taste sweet, slightly cold. Mainly supplements the five viscera, calms the spirit, settles the ethereal and corporeal souls (hun and po), stops fright palpitations, expels pathogenic qi, brightens the eyes, and opens the heart and sharpens the intellect. Also called Renxian or Guigai. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shihu (Dendrobium). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for internal injury; removes bi-syndrome, sends qi downward, and supplements the five viscera in cases of vacuity taxation and emaciation, and strengthens yin. Taken long-term, it thickens the stomach and intestines, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Also called Linlan. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shilongrui (Ranunculus sceleratus). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome and pathogenic qi in the heart and abdomen; benefits the joints and relieves vexation and fullness. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and prevents aging. Also called Luguoneng or Dishen. Grows in river marshes.

Niuxi (Achyranthes Root). Taste bitter (sour), nature neutral. Indicated for wilting and bi-syndrome from cold-damp, spasm and contracture of the limbs, and knee pain that prevents bending and stretching; expels stagnant blood and qi, treats burns and scalds, and induces abortion. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and resists aging. Also called Shibei. Grows in river valleys.

Duhuo (Doubleteeth Pubescent Angelica Root). Taste bitter, nature neutral, non-toxic. Indicated for conditions caused by wind-cold; treats wounds from metal blades, relieves pain, and treats running-piglet syndrome, epilepsy, tetany, and women's hernia-like abdominal masses. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and resists aging. Also called Qianghuo, Qiangqing, or Huqiang Shizhe. Grows in river valleys.

Shengma (Cimicifuga Root, Bugbane). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Mainly resolves the hundred poisons, kills the hundred kinds of ancient spirit-essences and calamity-bringing ghosts, and wards off epidemic pestilence, miasma, and gu-poison. Taken long-term, it prevents premature death, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Also called Zhou Shengma. Grows in mountain valleys.

Chaihu (Bupleurum Root). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for bound qi in the heart, abdomen, intestines, and stomach, accumulation of food and drink, and cold-heat pathogenic qi; expels the old and brings in the new. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and boosts essence. Also called Dixun. Grows in river valleys.

Fangkui. Taste pungent, nature cold. Indicated for hernia-like abdominal masses and intestinal diarrhea, bladder heat-binding with inhibited urination, cough with rising qi, warm malaria, epilepsy, and fright-mania with manic wandering. Taken long-term, it strengthens the bone marrow, boosts qi, and lightens the body. Also called Ligai. Grows in river valleys.

Shishi (Yarrow Seed, Achillea). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for impotence; boosts qi, fills out the flesh and skin, brightens the eyes, and sharpens intelligence and foresight. Taken long-term, it dispels hunger, prevents aging, and lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

Yanlvzi. Taste bitter, slightly warm, non-toxic. Indicated for stagnant blood in the five viscera, water-qi in the abdomen, abdominal distension with lingering heat, wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome, and various bodily pains. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prolongs life, and prevents aging. Grows in river valleys.

Yiyiren (Job's Tears, Coix Seed). Taste sweet, slightly cold. Indicated for tense sinews and contracture that prevent bending and stretching, and wind-damp bi-syndrome; sends qi downward. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and boosts qi. Its root expels the three worms. Also called Jieli. Grows in level marshes.

Cheqianzi (Plantago Seed). Taste sweet, nature cold, non-toxic. Indicated for qi dribbling-blockage; relieves pain, disinhibits the water passages and urination, and removes damp bi-syndrome. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and resists aging. Also called Dangtong. Grows in level marshes.
